title: The neuroscience of why meditation is harder for trauma survivors
author: Kalesh

The Body That Won't Settle

htmlContent:
The Body That Won't Settle

I sat on that meditation cushion for years. I tried every app, every timer, every guided voice telling me to breathe. And every single time, my body would revolt. My heart would race. My jaw would clench. My mind would spiral into places I did not want to go. I thought I was failing at meditation. I thought I was broken.

Here's the thing. I was not broken. My nervous system was doing exactly what it was designed to do. And if you have tried meditation and felt worse instead of better, I need you to hear this. You are not doing it wrong.

The science tells a different story than the wellness industry does. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to safety and threat. His work shows that meditation, in its traditional form, can actually trigger a survival response in people with trauma histories. Stay with me here.

When you sit still and close your eyes, your body does not automatically know this is safe. For someone with a trauma history, stillness can feel like vulnerability. Closing your eyes can feel like losing sight of danger. Deep breathing can feel like suffocation if your body associates slowed breath with being held down or silenced.

I remember the first time I tried a 10-day silent retreat. I lasted 36 hours. My nervous system went into full alarm. I was shaking, crying, and completely convinced I was going to die. The teachers told me to push through. They said it was resistance. They said I needed to sit with the discomfort.

They were wrong. Porges calls this "neuroception" - the way your nervous system scans for safety and threat below conscious awareness. When your neuroception detects danger, your body will not settle no matter how many times you count your breath. It cannot. That is not a character flaw. That is biology.

The Dorsal Vagal Trap

Here is where it gets more specific. Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has written extensively about how trauma survivors often have a nervous system that is stuck in a dorsal vagal state. That is the shutdown response. The collapse. The freeze.

When you sit down to meditate, you might not feel activated. You might feel numb. You might feel disconnected from your body entirely. You might feel like you are floating outside yourself. That is not enlightenment. That is dissociation.

Traditional meditation encourages you to notice your thoughts and let them go. But for trauma survivors, "letting go" can mean dissociating further. You are not processing anything. You are leaving your body because being in your body is too painful. Van der Kolk's research at the Trauma Center in Boston showed that many trauma survivors actually get worse with traditional mindfulness practices. Their dissociative symptoms increase. Their sense of disembodiment deepens.

Wild, right? The thing that is supposed to help you actually hurts you.

I spent years thinking I just needed to try harder. I bought the expensive cushions. I did the morning routines. I journaled about my meditation practice. None of it worked because I was asking a traumatized nervous system to do something it could not do. I was asking my body to feel safe when it had never learned what safety felt like.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is known for its ability to support relaxation without the gastrointestinal upset that other forms of magnesium can cause. For some trauma survivors, this kind of gentle nervous system support can make the difference between a practice that feels like torture and one that feels like rest.

The Problem with Stillness

Think about what meditation asks of you. It asks you to sit still. It asks you to be quiet. It asks you to turn your attention inward. For someone whose trauma involved being silenced, being frozen, or being trapped in their own body, these are not neutral instructions. These are triggers.

I worked with a client once who could not close her eyes during meditation without seeing her abuser's face. Every time she tried, her body would go rigid. Her breathing would become shallow. She would feel like she was back in that room. She thought she was crazy. She was not crazy. She was having a normal trauma response to an activity that her nervous system interpreted as dangerous.

Pat Ogden, the founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, has written extensively about how trauma lives in the body. She talks about how the body's movements, postures, and sensations hold the history of what has happened to us. When you sit still, you are asking those stored experiences to stay quiet. But they do not want to stay quiet. They want to be heard.

So what do you do if traditional meditation does not work for you? You stop doing traditional meditation. You find something that actually works with your nervous system, not against it.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. Levine developed Somatic Experiencing, which is a trauma therapy that works with the body's natural survival responses. His approach involves pendulation - gently moving between activation and settling - rather than forcing stillness. It is a completely different framework from traditional meditation, and it works for many people who cannot sit still.

Movement as Meditation

Here is what I learned. Meditation does not have to mean sitting still. It does not have to mean closing your eyes. It does not have to mean focusing on your breath.

For trauma survivors, meditation might look like walking. It might look like swaying. It might look like stretching. It might look like keeping your eyes open and focusing on a point in the room. It might look like humming or chanting. It might look like putting on music and letting your body move however it wants to move.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "mindsight," talks about how the brain integrates experience through rhythmic, patterned activity. That can be breath. But it can also be walking. It can be swimming. It can be rocking in a chair. It can be swinging on a swing. The key is rhythm and safety, not stillness and silence.

I now practice what I call "open-eyed meditation." I sit in a chair with my feet on the floor. I keep my eyes open and let them rest on something neutral - a plant, a wall, a candle flame. I let my breath be whatever it wants to be. Sometimes it is deep. Sometimes it is shallow. Sometimes I yawn. Sometimes I sigh. I do not force anything. I just let my body do what it needs to do while I stay present.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. This book goes deeper into how the body holds trauma and how gentle, mindful movement can help release it. It is not a meditation manual. It is a guide to understanding why your body reacts the way it does and how to work with those reactions instead of against them.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. This book explores how trauma can be passed down through generations and how family history can shape your nervous system's responses. It helped me understand why my body reacted to things that had never happened to me personally.

Redefining What Works

I am not saying meditation is bad. I am saying that the way it is often taught does not account for trauma. And that is a problem because trauma is incredibly common. The Adverse Childhood Experiences study showed that nearly two-thirds of adults have experienced at least one traumatic event in childhood. That means most people who sit down to meditate are doing so with a nervous system that is already on high alert.

So here is my invitation. Stop trying to force your body into a state it cannot access. Stop judging yourself for not being able to sit still. Stop believing that you are somehow less spiritual or less healed because meditation feels impossible.

Instead, ask your body what it needs. Does it need to move? Does it need to rock? Does it need to keep its eyes open? Does it need to hum? Does it need to feel the ground beneath its feet? Does it need to hold something soft? Does it need to be in a room with the lights on?

Your body is not your enemy. It is trying to protect you. And when you stop fighting it, you can start finding practices that actually work.

I still do not sit still for long. I still keep my eyes open. I still let my body move if it needs to. And I have stopped calling it meditation. I call it settling. I call it orienting. I call it coming home to myself.

That is not failure. That is wisdom.
